title: Search Variations: First Match, All Matches & Min/Max
description: Adapt the linear search pattern to find the first occurrence, every occurrence, and the minimum or maximum value in an array.
congratsText: Great work! You now know how to adapt linear search into several useful variations.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Find the first occurrence of a value in an array with duplicates
  • Find and report every occurrence of a value
  • Adapt linear search to find the minimum and maximum values
  • Recognize these as variations of one shared pattern

sections.title: One Prize or Many?

quiz.question: If more than one student shares the same top score, what question does that raise for a search function?
options: Should the search stop at the first match, or find every match?
Whether the array needs to be declared with a larger size
Whether linear search can even be used with duplicate values
Whether the array must be converted into a 2D array first

sections.lesson:
One Prize or Many?
Real data often has duplicates. The same base search loop can answer two very different questions: 'where is the FIRST match?' or 'where are ALL the matches?' — the choice depends on what the problem actually needs.

sections.title: Variation 1: First Match (Same as Basic Search)

quiz.question: What does firstMatch() return for target 20 in arr = {10, 20, 30, 20, 50}?
options: 1, the index of the first occurrence
3, the index of the last occurrence
2, the total number of matches
20, the value itself

sections.lesson:
Variation 1: First Match (Same as Basic Search)
Finding the first match is exactly Week 6A's search function — it already stops at the first hit and returns immediately, ignoring any duplicates later in the array.

sections.title: Variation 2: Find All Matches

quiz.question: What is the key structural change needed to find ALL matches instead of just the first?
options: Remove the early return so the loop keeps going after a match
Add a second nested loop that repeats the search
Change the comparison from == to !=
Sort the array before starting the loop

sections.lesson:
Variation 2: Find All Matches
Removing the early return is the entire change. Without it, the loop keeps scanning past a match instead of stopping, reporting every index where the target appears.
void findAll(int arr[], int size, int target) { for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] == target) { printf("Found at index %d\n", i); } } } // no return inside the if — the loop keeps going

sections.title: The Early-Return Bug

quiz.question: What goes wrong if findAll() accidentally keeps a return statement inside the if block?
options: It stops after the first match, missing every later occurrence
It prints every index in the array, matching or not
The program fails to compile because return is misplaced
It searches the array in reverse order instead of forward

sections.lesson:
The Early-Return Bug
This is one of the most common bugs in a find-all function: leaving a return (or break) inside the if block silently turns 'find all' back into 'find first', and later matches are missed without any error message.

sections.title: Variation 3: Finding the Minimum

quiz.question: What is the key difference between finding the minimum and searching for a target?
options: Elements are compared to each other, not to a fixed target value
The loop must always start at index 1 instead of index 0
The array must be sorted in ascending order beforehand
It requires exactly the same code as firstMatch()

sections.lesson:
Variation 3: Finding the Minimum
Instead of comparing every element to a fixed target, findMin() compares elements to each other, keeping track of the smallest one seen so far.
int findMin(int arr[], int size) { int min = arr[0]; // start by assuming the first is smallest for (int i = 1; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] < min) { min = arr[i]; } } return min; }

sections.title: Why the Loop Starts at Index 1 for Min/Max

quiz.question: Why does findMin() initialize min = arr[0] and then start its loop at i = 1?
options: The first element is already accounted for as the starting guess
Index 0 is invalid and must always be skipped in any array loop
It makes the function run in exactly half the time
C requires min/max loops to always begin at index 1

sections.lesson:
Why the Loop Starts at Index 1 for Min/Max
Since min already holds arr[0] before the loop begins, comparing it against arr[0] again inside the loop would be redundant. Starting at index 1 simply avoids that wasted, unnecessary comparison.

sections.title: Complete Example: findMax() by Adapting findMin()

quiz.question: What single change turns findMin() into findMax()?
options: Flipping the comparison operator from < to >
Changing the loop to start at index 0 instead of 1
Adding a second array parameter to the function
Reversing the array before the loop begins

sections.lesson:
Complete Example: findMax() by Adapting findMin()
Just like descending sort will later flip one symbol, findMax() is findMin() with the comparison flipped from < to >. The overall shape stays identical.
int findMax(int arr[], int size) { int max = arr[0]; for (int i = 1; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] > max) { max = arr[i]; } } return max; }

sections.title: Best Practice: One Shared Array, Three Tests

quiz.question: Why is it useful to test firstMatch(), findAll(), and findMinMax() against the same shared test array?
options: It makes it easy to compare each function's results and confirm they're all working correctly against known data
C requires all functions in a file to use identical input
It reduces the number of variables the program needs to declare
It's only useful if the array contains no duplicate values

sections.lesson:
Best Practice: One Shared Array, Three Tests
Testing all three variations against the same dataset makes results easy to verify by eye and easy to compare — if the shared array is known in advance, you already know what each function should return.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: What do first-match, find-all, and min/max all have in common?
options: They are all variations on the same loop-and-compare pattern from basic linear search
They all require the array to be sorted before running
They each need a completely different, unrelated algorithm
They only work on arrays containing exactly five elements

sections.lesson:
Recap
All three variations reuse the same loop-and-compare shape from basic linear search — only the comparison and what happens on a match change. Removing an early return finds every match; comparing elements to each other instead of a target finds the min or max.
int findMax(int arr[], int size) { int max = arr[0]; for (int i = 1; i < size; i++) if (arr[i] > max) max = arr[i]; return max; }
